sanskrit:
किं पुनर्ब्राह्मणा: पुण्या भक्ता राजर्षयस्तथा।
अनित्यमसुखं लोकमिमं प्राप्य भजस्व माम्॥
transliteration:
kiṃ punar brāhmaṇāḥ puṇyā bhaktā rājarṣayas tathā |
anityam asukhaṃ lokam imaṃ prāpya bhajasva mām ||
literal_meaning: How much more then the virtuous brāhmaṇas, devotees, and royal sages—having attained this temporary, sorrowful world—do engage in loving service to Me.
contextual_meaning: Even high-status, morally accomplished people feel the same existential ache; their status does not exempt them from impermanence and distress. The verse reframes achievement as a reminder to pivot from external validation to internal devotion, turning worldly success into a launching pad for transcendence rather than a resting place for ego.
psychological_state: Post-achievement let-down: the moment when accolades, wealth, or titles no longer mask the gut-level knowledge that nothing here lasts or fully satisfies.
trigger_scenario: You just closed on your dream house, posted the photos, and still feel hollow inside; the dopamine spike fades and the mortgage looms.
anchor_text: Let success humble you into seeking the eternal, not seduce you into clinging to the fleeting.
